80. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.7

Finding key: 29985e78585abc194cd32a1f08539e48a4aee1a5f292ffc732d8092d0af517b9

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

5.1.7 A DAR needs to be specific as to the personal data that is being sought. In this context, a request for “all personal data” shall not be considered to be a proper DAR.

Context: 5.1.7 A DAR needs to be specific as to the personal data that is being sought. In this context, a request for “all personal data” shall not be considered to be a proper DAR.

Rationale: This section requires DARs to specify the personal data sought and rejects blanket requests. It maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral data-access rule, not a comprehensive horizontal personal-data-protection framework.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.1

81. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.12

Finding key: fe6af03170a183aca486a24adec7d7f551304df1867ad4774765cc82dcdd742e

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

In instances where a Data User receives a verbal request for access to personal data, the Data User is not required to respond to the request.

Context: 5.1.12 In instances where a Data User receives a verbal request for access to personal data, the Data User is not required to respond to the request. However, the Data User should guide the Requestor on the proper manner of making a valid DAR and provide whatever assistance as may be required by the Requestor to make a DAR. Receipt And Processing Of A DAR

Rationale: This section permits non-response to verbal access requests and guides DAR handling. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral personal-data framework, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.1

82. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.18

Finding key: 826e8037ada97f0dd96a6e37b766c5fe3f0462ad5c5281b5f2e40d6a227ecf37

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

5.1.18 In relation to the communication of video and audio personal data to Requestors who have made a DAR, Data Users may utilise the following means of communication as may be available to them:

Context: 5.1.18 In relation to the communication of video and audio personal data to Requestors who have made a DAR, Data Users may utilise the following means of communication as may be available to them: (i) audio recordings may be communicated as written transcripts or provided in audio form (e.g. .wmv or mpeg); and/or (ii) video recordings (inclusive of CCTV images) may be communicated as a chronological set of image captures which are then printed, or as an edited video recording where all other persons’ identities have been removed or masked. Refusal To Comply With A DAR

Rationale: This section permits communication of video/audio personal data in response to a DAR. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral personal-data framework for banking/financial institutions, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.1

83. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.19

Finding key: 968214602b0651cd9820d82f628ec7aced0660b8d4f4fcfea318bc924046e229

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

The Act recognises that Data Users may legitimately refuse to comply with a DAR in some circumstances as detailed in section 32 of the Act.

Context: 5.1.19 The Act recognises that Data Users may legitimately refuse to comply with a DAR in some circumstances as detailed in section 32 of the Act.

Rationale: This provision permits Data Users to refuse to comply with a DAR in specified circumstances. It maps to P7-I1 because it evidences the banking-sector data-protection framework, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

84. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.24

Finding key: 2cc7281c1ea7e2b72deb69dd33df6655b807598f472c0416acdd8ffccba24831

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

It is recommended that Data Users should maintain a file for each DAR with the following information:-

Context: 5.1.24 It is recommended that Data Users should maintain a file for each DAR with the following information:- (i) a copy of the DAR; (ii) a record of the verification of the identity of the Requestor; (iii) copies of all correspondences relevant to the DAR; (iv) a record of any decision made in relation to the DAR; and (v) a copy of the personal data that was sent to the Requestor in question.

Rationale: This section recommends that Data Users maintain DAR files with specified records. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral data-protection framework in banking/financial institutions, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.1

85. P7-I1 · s. 5.1.25

Finding key: d4270217b73f4f0cc57262a8ec4ae62e5ee9b11ccf0f25c398a3fafd220f700b

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

5.2 RIGHT TO CORRECT PERSONAL DATA

Context: 5.1.25 In handling DARs for joint accounts, the joint account mandate will apply. For example if the joint account mandate requires all account holders to sign, the DAR must be issued by all the joint account holders or by their jointly appointed Relevant Person. 5.2 RIGHT TO CORRECT PERSONAL DATA

Rationale: This section establishes the right to correct personal data. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a horizontal personal-data-protection framework’s core right, so the economy does not lack a framework in the absolute sense.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.1

86. P7-I1 · s. 5.2.12

Finding key: 10d181551c330d0b5f1332b8ed723421b30409299597ba2f227dda27532fb3e5

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Where appropriate, Data Users may request for supporting evidence from Requestors prior to effecting the change requested in their respective DCRs.

Context: 5.2.12 Where appropriate, Data Users may request for supporting evidence from Requestors prior to effecting the change requested in their respective DCRs.

Rationale: This section permits Data Users to request supporting evidence before changing DCRs. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral data-protection framework in banking, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

87. P7-I1 · s. 5.2.16

Finding key: c4a62d7eaefc4b38a4a6e42cc747c29c50b8a4d9b516ecbea7500084da13883f

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

It is recommended that Data Users should maintain a file for each DCR with the following information:- (i) a copy of the DCR; (ii) a record of the verification of the identity of the Requestor; (iii) copies of all correspondences relevant to the DCR; (iv) a record of any decision made in relation to the DCR; and (v) a copy of the corrected personal data that was sent to the Requestor in question.

Context: 5.2.16 It is recommended that Data Users should maintain a file for each DCR with the following information:- (i) a copy of the DCR; (ii) a record of the verification of the identity of the Requestor; (iii) copies of all correspondences relevant to the DCR; (iv) a record of any decision made in relation to the DCR; and (v) a copy of the corrected personal data that was sent to the Requestor in question.

Rationale: This section recommends recordkeeping by Data Users for each DCR. It evidences a sectoral personal-data framework because it establishes a data-correction request procedure and related obligations, but it is not a comprehensive horizontal DP law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.2

88. P7-I1 · s. 5.3.2

Finding key: d559e23ea585516047f3c8ead33d1c5a6b17f55b6cd743a1bf933b1afb709109

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

“unwarranted” means that the damage or distress suffered by the Data Subject or another person is not justifiable.

Context: 5.3.2 The Act does not define what is meant by “unwarranted” and “substantial damage or distress”. However, in most cases: PDP Code Of Practice For The Banking And Financial Sector Part 5 58 (i) “substantial damage” includes financial loss or physical harm suffered by the Data Subject or another person; (ii) “substantial distress” includes emotional or mental pain suffered by the Data Subject or another person; and (iii) “unwarranted” means that the damage or distress suffered by the Data Subject or another person is not justifiable.

Rationale: This provision establishes an interpretive definition for the Act’s threshold terms. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences the banking-sector data protection framework’s operation, and ESCAP records such framework provisions as evidence even though the score reflects absence/comprehensiveness.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.3

89. P7-I1 · s. 5.3.4

Finding key: eb70d934b51441a18fbb24ec97e4b2281e08525bb7f13283ca5a94fe7d9adbd8

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Upon receiving a Data Subject Notice, the Data User is required to, within twenty one (21) days of such receipt, provide the Data Subject concerned with a written notice

Context: 5.3.4 Upon receiving a Data Subject Notice, the Data User is required to, within twenty one (21) days of such receipt, provide the Data Subject concerned with a written notice:- (i) stating that the Data User has complied with or intends to comply with the Data Subject Notice; or (ii) if the Data User does not intend to comply with the Data Subject Notice, to provide reasons for the decision; or (iii) stating reasons why the Data User finds the Data Subject Notice unjustified or to any extent unjustified and the extent to which the Data User has complied or intends to comply (if any). PDP Code Of Practice For The Banking And Financial Sector Part 5 59

Rationale: This section requires Data Users to provide a written notice in response to a Data Subject Notice. It maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral data-protection framework, not a comprehensive horizontal law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.3

90. P7-I1 · s. 5.4.1

Finding key: 26ca5b086c013901444de6355ec92a962c6a923affc24e01034809318674a939

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

A Data Subject has the right to withdraw his/her consent for the processing of his/her personal data, at any time, by providing the Data User with a written notice.

Context: 5.4.1 A Data Subject has the right to withdraw his/her consent for the processing of his/her personal data, at any time, by providing the Data User with a written notice. However, this right is restricted to instances where consent has been given by the Data Subject.

Rationale: This section establishes a right to withdraw consent for personal-data processing. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a personal-data-protection framework in the banking sector, even though the indicator scores absence and the law is sectoral, not comprehensive.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.4

91. P7-I1 · s. 5.4.3

Finding key: db6408be25cb6c7917c5bc8734cf8f173d5764a2a05ca33f2dfde1ee8de09fde

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

The following are examples of situations in which a Data Subject cannot withdraw his/her consent to the processing of personal data:

Context: 5.4.3 The following are examples of situations in which a Data Subject cannot withdraw his/her consent to the processing of personal data: PDP Code Of Practice For The Banking And Financial Sector Part 5 60 (i) Where the personal data is required for the performance of a contract between the Data User and Data Subject; Example 1: Where a Data Subject enters into an agreement with a Data User in order to secure a loan/financing or a credit card, and the Data User processes his/her personal data in relation to the loan/financing or credit card, the Data Subject is not entitled to withdraw his/her consent to process personal data necessary for the performance of the contract as Section 6(2)(a) would be applicable in this instance. Example 2: Where a Data User follows up with the Data Subject for overdue payments or issues letters of demand to the Data Subject or commences legal proceedings against the Data Subject in order to recover the monies owed, the Data Subject is not entitled to withdraw his/her consent for the processing of his/her personal data as the recovery of monies by the Data User is part of the performance of the contract, and as such Section 6(2)(a) would be applicable in this instance. (ii) Where the personal data has been provided by the Data Subject to the Data User in order that the Data User may fulfil a pre-contractual request of the Data Subject; Example: Where the Data Subject makes an application with the Data User for the opening of an account or for the provision of facility or services, and the Data User conducts the necessary pre-contractual due diligence credit, anti-money laundering and risk management checks, the Data Subject is not entitled to withdraw his/her consent to process personal data as Section 6(2)(b) of the Act would be applicable in this instance. (iii) Where the Data User is required to comply with any non-contractual legal obligation that the Data User is subject to. Example 1: Where the Data User is required to provide personal data of Data Subjects to Bank Negara Malaysia, Inland Revenue or other law enforcement authorities in order to comply with the regulatory reporting requirements of the Anti-Money Laundering and Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001, the Data Subject is not entitled to withdraw his/her consent to process personal data as Section 6(2)(c) of the Act would be applicable in this instance. Example 2: Where the processing of personal data is for purposes of compliance with relevant BNM’s Guideline pursuant to powers conferred to BNM by the Financial Services Act, the Data Subject is not entitled to PDP Code Of Practice For The Banking And Financial Sector Part 5 61 withdraw his/her consent to process personal data as Section 6(2)(c) of the Act would be applicable in this instance.

Rationale: This provision establishes a limitation on withdrawal of consent for processing personal data. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a sectoral banking data-protection framework, which is recorded as the framework’s existence rather than a comprehensive horizontal law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.4

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

5.5.3 The permitted practices of the banking and financial sector are addressed in greater depth in Part 4 of this Code.

93. P7-I1 · s. 5.5.6

Finding key: 9f7910457084e917877293f4f2a9a51c262e9e8f0bc692df1854cfcf3be55892

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Data Users are permitted to not provide Data Subjects with all direct marketing materials

Context: 5.5.6 Where Data Subjects make a written request to Data Users stating their choice to receive some direct marketing materials and not others (e.g. direct marketing materials for credit card offers and not property loans/financing), Data Users are permitted to not provide Data Subjects with all direct marketing materials (barring marketing communications that are not specifically targeted as addressed in 4.4.3), should their systems be incapable of distinguishing between the differing types of financial products so marketed. PDP Code Of Practice For The Banking And Financial Sector Part 6 64 PART 6 EMPLOYEES 6.1 POLICIES AND PROCEDURES DEVELOPMENT

Rationale: This section permits a sectoral data-user rule on direct marketing. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a banking-sector privacy framework, but it is not a comprehensive horizontal personal-data-protection law.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5.5

94. P7-I1 · s. 5(2)

Finding key: 43ba7fdf280f40a06d829f845bae76fd48ee3a8ef75eff7304f13312a09f93eb

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Subject to sections 45 and 46, a data user who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence

Context: (2) Subject to sections 45 and 46, a data user who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both. General Principle

Rationale: This section prohibits contravention of the General Principle by a data user. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences the horizontal personal-data-protection framework’s core obligation, which ESCAP records as framework existence.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5 › item (2)

95. P7-I1 · s. 5

Finding key: 24a318a196b01f24b325c5ad49e63340c90c88a1fdaec1bb46134061748c9479

Law: Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1727) · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

dengan menggantikan perkataan “pengguna data hendaklah,” dengan perkataan “pengawal data dan seseorang pemproses data hendaklah,”

Context: Seksyen 9 Akta ibu dipinda— (a) dalam subseksyen (1), dengan menggantikan perkataan “pengguna data hendaklah,” dengan perkataan “pengawal data dan seseorang pemproses data hendaklah,”; dan (b) dalam subseksyen (2)— (i) dengan menggantikan perkataan “pengguna data, pengguna data itu hendaklah,” dengan perkataan “seorang pengawal data, pemproses data itu hendaklah,”; (ii) dengan memotong perkataan “, memastikan bahawa pemproses data itu”; dan Undang-Undang Malaysia 6 Akta A1727 (iii) dalam teks bahasa Inggeris— (A) dalam perenggan (a), dengan menggantikan perkataan “provides” dengan perkataan “provide”; dan (B) dalam perenggan (b), dengan menggantikan perkataan “takes” dengan perkataan “take”. Penggal baharu 1a Bahagian II

Rationale: This amendment requires data controllers and processors to comply with s. 9 obligations. It maps to P7-I1 because it evidences the existence of a horizontal personal-data-protection framework, not the absence of one.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 5

96. P7-I1 · s. 9(1)

Finding key: a831a6c7d5a925bccdfeb536711998a6b18beacea444c08b381fff1bb1e2ae0f

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

A data user shall, when processing personal data, take practical steps to protect the personal data from any loss, misuse, modification, unauthorized or accidental access or disclosure, alteration or destruction

Context: (1) A data user shall, when processing personal data, take practical steps to protect the personal data from any loss, misuse, modification, unauthorized or accidental access or disclosure, alteration or destruction by having regard— (a) to the nature of the personal data and the harm that would result from such loss, misuse, modification, unauthorized or accidental access or disclosure, alteration or destruction; (b) to the place or location where the personal data is stored; (c) to any security measures incorporated into any equipment in which the personal data is stored; (d) to the measures taken for ensuring the reliability, integrity and competence of personnel having access to the personal data; and (e) to the measures taken for ensuring the secure transfer of the personal data.

Rationale: This section requires data users to take practical steps to protect personal data during processing. It evidences a horizontal core data-protection obligation (security safeguards), supporting existence of a comprehensive framework for P7-I1.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 9 › item (1)

Status evidence
{

97. P7-I1 · s. 7(1)

Finding key: 2d4e7793e198b6cb5741031224be06e716a3f6e73788ab648f20eec6cb70aef3

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

A data user shall by written notice inform a data subject—

Context: (1) A data user shall by written notice inform a data subject— (a) that personal data of the data subject is being processed by or on behalf of the data user, and shall provide a description of the personal data to that data subject; (b) the purposes for which the personal data is being or is to be collected and further processed; (c) of any information available to the data user as to the source of that personal data; (d) of the data subject’s right to request access to and to request correction of the personal data and how to contact the data user with any inquiries or complaints in respect of the personal data; 19 Personal Data Protection (e) of the class of third parties to whom the data user discloses or may disclose the personal data; (f) of the choices and means the data user offers the data subject for limiting the processing of personal data, including personal data relating to other persons who may be identified from that personal data; (g) whether it is obligatory or voluntary for the data subject to supply the personal data; and (h) where it is obligatory for the data subject to supply the personal data, the consequences for the data subject if he fails to supply the personal data.

Rationale: This section requires a data user to provide written notice to a data subject. It evidences core horizontal data-protection obligations (notice and data-subject rights), supporting existence of a comprehensive personal-data-protection framework.

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 7 › item (1)

98. P7-I1 · s. 45(2)

Finding key: 70e44201336a5f9a24cd7a67e85b6e8136ede17f2b1c8a24437b94046ec6eaa9

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

personal data— (a) processed for— (i) the prevention or detection of crime or for the purpose of investigations; (ii) the apprehension or prosecution of offenders; or (iii) the assessment or collection of any tax or duty or any other imposition of a similar nature, shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle

Context: (2) Subject to section 46, personal data— (a) processed for— (i) the prevention or detection of crime or for the purpose of investigations; (ii) the apprehension or prosecution of offenders; or (iii) the assessment or collection of any tax or duty or any other imposition of a similar nature, shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act; (b) processed in relation to information of the physical or mental health of a data subject shall be exempted from the Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act of which the application of the provisions to the data subject would be likely to cause serious harm to the physical or mental health of the data subject or any other individual; (c) processed for preparing statistics or carrying out research shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act, provided that such personal data is not processed for any other purpose and that the resulting statistics or the results of the research are not made available in a form which identifies the data subject; (d) that is necessary for the purpose of or in connection with any order or judgement of a court shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act; 46 ACT 709 Laws of Malaysia (e) processed for the purpose of discharging regulatory functions shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act if the application of those provisions to the personal data would be likely to prejudice the proper discharge of those functions; or (f) processed only for journalistic, literary or artistic purposes shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle, Retention Principle, Data Integrity Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act, provided that— (i) the processing is undertaken with a view to the publication by any person of the journalistic, literary or artistic material; (ii) the data user reasonably believes that, taking into account the special importance of public interest in freedom of expression, the publication would be in the public interest; and (iii) the data user reasonably believes that in all the circumstances, compliance with the provision in respect of which the exemption is claimed is incompatible with the journalistic, literary or artistic purposes. Power to make further exemptions

Rationale: This section provides exemptions from multiple core data-protection principles for specified processing purposes. Maps to P7-I1 because it evidences a horizontal personal-data-protection framework with core principles and controlling exemptions.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 45 › item (2)

99. P7-I2 · s. 27(1)

Finding key: 1d3d0f5dd6ea402aa36029de55e9dc1c3c1ba1bad4f794f6e15a740af021865b

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

No person shall— (a) provide any cyber security service; or (b) advertise, or in any way hold himself out as a provider of a cyber security service, unless he holds a licence to provide a cyber security service issued under this Part.

Context: (1) No person shall— (a) provide any cyber security service; or (b) advertise, or in any way hold himself out as a provider of a cyber security service, unless he holds a licence to provide a cyber security service issued under this Part.

Rationale: This section prohibits providing or advertising cyber security services without a licence. It establishes a dedicated licensing control for cyber security services, which evidences a cybersecurity legal framework for P7-I2.

103. P7-I2 · s. 27(3)

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

(3) This Part shall not apply in the case where the cyber security service is provided by a company to its related company.

Context: (3) This Part shall not apply in the case where the cyber security service is provided by a company to its related company.

Rationale: This section excludes certain intra-group cyber security services from the Part’s application. Maps to P7-I2 because it evidences the Cyber Security Act’s cybersecurity framework, which is a dedicated horizontal framework rather than an absence of one.

(5) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both.

Context: (5) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or to both. Qualifications for application of licence

Official source · Status: in_force

the period and manner in which the information relating to a cyber security incident shall be notified to the Chief Executive and the national critical information infrastructure sector lead;

Context: (2) Without prejudice to the generality of subsection (1), the Minister may make regulations to prescribe— (a) the period for the conduct of a cyber security risk assessment and the carrying out of audit; (b) the period and manner in which the information relating to a cyber security incident shall be notified to the Chief Executive and the national critical information infrastructure sector lead; (c) the cyber security service in respect of which a licence shall be obtained under this Act before it can be provided; (d) the manner for the application of licence and renewal of licence under this Act; (e) the fees payable under this Act; (f) the offences which may be compounded and the forms to be used and the method and procedure for compounding the offences under this Act; (g) any other matters required by this Act to be prescribed.

Rationale: This section permits the Minister to make regulations prescribing incident-notification period and manner to the Chief Executive and the national critical information infrastructure sector lead. Maps to P7-I2 because it evidences a dedicated cybersecurity incident-response notification framework (re

108. P7-I2 · s. 35(2)

Finding key: 3bb1713b5a640d25ec21c250c416366b2592f0f822a762107b68a0faccbb809c

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

(2) The investigation carried out under this Part shall be for the purposes of— (a) ascertaining whether a cyber security incident has occurred; or (b) in the case where a cyber security incident has occurred, determining the measures necessary to respond to or recover from the cyber security incident and preventing such cyber security incident from occurring in the future.

Context: (2) The investigation carried out under this Part shall be for the purposes of— (a) ascertaining whether a cyber security incident has occurred; or (b) in the case where a cyber security incident has occurred, determining the measures necessary to respond to or recover from the cyber security incident and preventing such cyber security incident from occurring in the future. Laws of Malaysia 34 Act 854

Rationale: This section mandates the purposes of investigations under the Act: ascertain incidents, determine response/recovery measures, and prevent recurrence. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity incident-response framework function (investigation/response/recovery/prevention).

(1) The Chief Executive shall establish and maintain a national cyber security system known as the “National Cyber Coordination and Command Centre System” for the purpose of dealing with cyber security threats and cyber security incidents.

Context: (1) The Chief Executive shall establish and maintain a national cyber security system known as the “National Cyber Coordination and Command Centre System” for the purpose of dealing with cyber security threats and cyber security incidents.

110. P7-I2 · s. 22(1)

Finding key: 072597dca9a59c2a2b5e77a0bf106a1c6557b933e8f51b6f75c7a9a8e2d558c0

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

A national critical information infrastructure entity shall within the period as may be prescribed— (a) conduct a cyber security risk assessment in respect of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity in accordance with the code of practice and directive; and (b) cause to be carried out an audit by an auditor appro

Context: (1) A national critical information infrastructure entity shall within the period as may be prescribed— (a) conduct a cyber security risk assessment in respect of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity in accordance with the code of practice and directive; and (b) cause to be carried out an audit by an auditor approved by the Chief Executive to determine the compliance of the national critical information infrastructure entity with this Act.

Rationale: This section requires national critical information infrastructure entities to conduct cyber security risk assessments and commission compliance audits under prescribed timelines. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical infrastructure (core obligations under the Cyber Security

111. P7-I2 · s. 26

Finding key: ce6c7757c870e304a6ef517927967dba2c32f1e1832605c70169c4169b71ae93

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

Where a national critical information infrastructure sector lead directs any national critical information infrastructure entity to implement measures, standards and processes to ensure the cyber security of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity under any other written law, the national critical information inf

Context: Where a national critical information infrastructure sector lead directs any national critical information infrastructure entity to implement measures, standards and processes to ensure the cyber security of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity under any other written law, the national critical information infrastructure sector lead shall ensure that the directions given are consistent with the code of practice. Cyber Security 29 Part VI CYBER SECURITY SERVICE PROVIDER Licensing of cyber security service provider

Rationale: This section requires the sector lead, when directing CI entities to implement cyber-security measures under other laws, to ensure the directions are consistent with the code of practice. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical information infrastructure (CI).

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 26

Status evidence
{

112. P7-I2 · s. 22(5)

Finding key: e3ed8a5f8d758a9febf5e5fb6899a30b04ed959f6f3796b5ed7fe26ab0d82cf0

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

the Chief Executive may by notice in writing direct the national critical information infrastructure entity which owns or operates the national critical information infrastructure to conduct a cyber security risk assessment or cause to be carried out an audit

Context: (5) Upon receipt of the information from the national critical information infrastructure sector lead under subsection 20(5) on the material change made to the design, configuration, security or operation of a national critical information infrastructure, the Chief Executive may by notice in writing direct the national critical information infrastructure entity which owns or operates the national critical information infrastructure to conduct a cyber security risk assessment or cause to be carried out an audit in respect of the national critical information infrastructure regardless whether a cyber security risk assessment or audit has been conducted or carried out under subsection (1) in respect of such national critical information infrastructure. Laws of Malaysia 26 Act 854

Rationale: This section permits the Chief Executive to direct CIIOs to conduct cyber security risk assessments or audits. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity incident/risk-assessment control framework for critical infrastructure, supporting P7-I2 (cybersecurity framework existence).

113. P7-I2 · s. 25(1)

Finding key: 4562ccad1a66e39eff417c44c423ebd5c8eadc6156b38114ad29be012d91c11b

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

A national critical information infrastructure sector lead shall prepare a code of practice to be endorsed by the Chief Executive containing measures, standards and processes in ensuring the cyber security of a national critical information infrastructure within the national critical information infrastructure sector for which it is appointed.

Context: (1) A national critical information infrastructure sector lead shall prepare a code of practice to be endorsed by the Chief Executive containing measures, standards and processes in ensuring the cyber security of a national critical information infrastructure within the national critical information infrastructure sector for which it is appointed.

Rationale: This section requires a national critical information infrastructure sector lead to prepare and seek endorsement of a cyber-security code of practice for its sector. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical information infrastructure sectors (legal function: code-of-practice fra

114. P7-I2 · s. 35(4)

Finding key: 92d083a852935faaab3cb3b6654d1cfd8e4e19df51af5a2225df1ea28e1ab334

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

Upon being notified by the authorized officer under paragraph (3)(b) that a cyber security incident has occurred, the Chief Executive may issue a directive to the national critical information infrastructure entity which owns or operates the national critical information infrastructure concerned

Context: (4) Upon being notified by the authorized officer under paragraph (3)(b) that a cyber security incident has occurred, the Chief Executive may issue a directive to the national critical information infrastructure entity which owns or operates the national critical information infrastructure concerned on the measures necessary to respond to or recover from the cyber security incident and to prevent such cyber security incident from occurring in the future.

Rationale: This section permits the Chief Executive, after notification of a cyber security incident, to issue directives to national critical information infrastructure entities on response/recovery and future prevention. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity incident response framework for critical infrastr

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

submit the cyber security risk assessment report or audit report to the Chief Executive.

Context: (2) The national critical information infrastructure entity shall, within the period of thirty days after the completion of the cyber security risk assessment or audit under subsection (1), submit the cyber security risk assessment report or audit report to the Chief Executive.

Rationale: This section requires the national critical information infrastructure entity to submit its cyber security risk assessment/audit report to the Chief Executive within a set period. 116. P7-I2 · s. 35(1)

Finding key: 790fcbff39ec2f80bdd688fa5c6120ba628a5b64dfdcbfb8df9ebf98c770436b

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

Upon receipt of the notification on cyber security incident from a national critical information infrastructure entity under section 23 or if it comes to the knowledge of the Chief Executive that a cyber security incident in respect of a national critical information infrastructure has or might have occurred, the Chief Executive shall instruct an authorized officer to investigate into the matter.

Context: (1) Upon receipt of the notification on cyber security incident from a national critical information infrastructure entity under section 23 or if it comes to the knowledge of the Chief Executive that a cyber security incident in respect of a national critical information infrastructure has or might have occurred, the Chief Executive shall instruct an authorized officer to investigate into the matter.

Rationale: This section requires the Chief Executive to instruct an authorized officer to investigate a cyber security incident involving a national critical information infrastructure, triggered by notification or the Chief Executive’s knowledge. It evidences an incident-response framework element (investigat

Finding key: 24949716ef57bbc9399232ceab5146bc477d125ea084301c252fe53081d2f920

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

A national critical information infrastructure entity shall implement the measures, standards and processes as specified in the code of practice to ensure the cyber security of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity.

Context: (1) A national critical information infrastructure entity shall implement the measures, standards and processes as specified in the code of practice to ensure the cyber security of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity.

Rationale: This section requires CII entities to implement cyber-security measures via a code of practice. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical information infrastructure (core obligation + controlling framework instrument).

118. P7-I2 · s. 21(4)

Finding key: b0d10c87f954557c15b644b08d5c72f733cd62fbe21e69fe1d78ae9e2ee1d23e

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

provided that such measures, standards and processes are not in contravention with the code of practice.

Context: (4) Where a national critical information infrastructure entity implements measures, standards and processes to ensure the cyber security of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity as required under any other written law, the national critical information infrastructure entity shall be deemed to have complied with this section provided that such measures, standards and processes are not in contravention with the code of practice.

Rationale: This section deems compliance for national critical information infrastructure entities implementing cyber security measures/standards/processes under other written law, unless they contravene the code of practice. Legal function: incident/cyber-risk compliance framework for critical infrastructure,

Finding key: 63a7e4258389cc4b3d2f3814623dd19a5d6ea967e125d055d382687a065ed881

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

the Chief Executive may direct the national critical information infrastructure entity to take further initiatives to re-evaluate the cyber security risk to such national critical information infrastructure

Context: (3) Where it appears to the Chief Executive from the cyber security risk assessment report submitted under subsection (2) that the result of the cyber security risk assessment is not satisfactory, the Chief Executive may direct the national critical information infrastructure entity to take further initiatives to re-evaluate the cyber security risk to such national critical information infrastructure within the period as may be determined by the Chief Executive.

Rationale: This section permits the Chief Executive to direct a national critical information infrastructure entity to re-evaluate cyber security risk. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical information infrastructure risk management/incident-related governance, so P7-I2 is not met (abse

120. P7-I2 · s. 23(1)

Finding key: 68cd821cd4e331624d9ec4b846b074b0c4f10c5795eed889a3d6bff95b8957f2

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

the national critical information infrastructure entity shall notify the Chief Executive and its national critical information infrastructure sector lead of such information

Context: (1) If it comes to the knowledge of a national critical information infrastructure entity that a cyber security incident has or might have occurred in respect of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity, the national critical information infrastructure entity shall notify the Chief Executive and its national critical information infrastructure sector lead of such information within the period and in such manner as may be prescribed.

Rationale: This section requires a national critical information infrastructure entity to notify the Chief Executive and its sector lead of a cyber security incident (or possible incident). Maps to P7-I2 because it evidences a dedicated critical-infrastructure incident notification framework.

A national critical information infrastructure entity may, in addition to the measures, standards and processes referred to in subsection (1) or (2), establish and implement the measures, standards and processes on cyber security based on internationally recognized standards or framework.

Context: (3) A national critical information infrastructure entity may, in addition to the measures, standards and processes referred to in subsection (1) or (2), establish and implement the measures, standards and processes on cyber security based on internationally recognized standards or framework.

122. P7-I2 · s. 16

Finding key: b9a6c9d0b1b9928d3d1e7f4262809b3b218cc3225b9ca7486f5ffe1878c99aa1

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

(d) to monitor and ensure that actions required of and duties imposed on the national critical information infrastructure entities under this Act are carried out by the national critical information infrastructure entities; (e) to prepare and maintain guidelines on best practices in relation to cyber security management;

Context: The national critical information infrastructure sector lead shall, in respect of the national critical information infrastructure sector for which it is appointed, have the following functions: (a) to designate a national critical information infrastructure entity under section 17; (b) to prepare a code of practice as required under section 25; (c) to implement the decisions of the Committee and directives under this Act; (d) to monitor and ensure that actions required of and duties imposed on the national critical information infrastructure entities under this Act are carried out by the national critical information infrastructure entities; (e) to prepare and maintain guidelines on best practices in relation to cyber security management; (f) to prepare and submit to the Chief Executive a situational report whether on its own initiative or as required by the Chief Executive where a cyber security threat or cyber security incident has affected a national critical information infrastructure within its national critical information infrastructure sector; and (g) to carry out such other functions under this Act. Designation of national critical information infrastructure entity

Rationale: This section requires the national critical information infrastructure sector lead to monitor compliance and ensure duties are carried out, and to prepare/maintain cyber security best-practice guidelines. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity framework for critical infrastructure incident/cyber-sec

123. P7-I2 · s. 20(3)

Finding key: ce084659a84b8b5791c5f9db1cdd9052a4cb9ec3a3e795285eaea23c44ba1acb

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

the national critical information infrastructure entity shall notify its national critical information infrastructure sector lead of the material change within thirty days from the date the change was completed.

Context: (3) If a material change is made to the design, configuration, security or operation of the national critical information infrastructure owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure entity after the information on such national critical information infrastructure has been provided under subsection (1), the national critical information infrastructure entity shall notify its national critical information infrastructure sector lead of the material change within thirty days from the date the change was completed.

Rationale: This section requires notification of material changes to national critical information infrastructure to the sector lead within 30 days. Maps to P7-I2 because it evidences a dedicated critical-infrastructure cybersecurity governance/incident-change reporting framework.

124. P7-I2 · s. 18(4)

Finding key: a090326f05d4d1e240d1480c939fc7347e94ef3479111cbed5932397aa22f92f

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

shall by notice in writing notify the Chief Executive without delay if— (a) the national critical information infrastructure sector lead is of the opinion that the computer or computer system owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure sector lead has ceased to be a national critical information infrastructure; or (b) the national critical information infrastructure secto

Context: (4) The national critical information infrastructure sector lead which has been designated as a national critical information infrastructure entity under this section shall by notice in writing notify the Chief Executive without delay if— (a) the national critical information infrastructure sector lead is of the opinion that the computer or computer system owned or operated by the national critical information infrastructure sector lead has ceased to be a national critical information infrastructure; or (b) the national critical information infrastructure sector lead no longer owns or operates any national critical information infrastructure. Laws of Malaysia 22 Act 854 Revocation of designation of national critical information infrastructure entity

Rationale: This section requires the national critical information infrastructure sector lead to notify the Chief Executive without delay upon revocation/cessation of designation. It evidences a dedicated cybersecurity critical-infrastructure governance framework (incident/asset status reporting).

125. P7-I3 · s. 3.5.5

Finding key: ae767ad296e0ed107f5edeff2886541feb152f256078226a2087008e98facb56

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

However, Data Users are allowed to retain such personal data for more than seven (7) years from the date of non-approval, closure or termination of the said accounts/facilities

Context: 3.5.5 However, Data Users are allowed to retain such personal data for more than seven (7) years from the date of non-approval, closure or termination of the said accounts/facilities in the following circumstances: (i) if it is required by the law; (ii) if it is an obligation under the law for a Data User to report, disclose or verify a Data Subject’s details, in which case the retention period allowed in the said circumstances shall be until the disposal of the matter; (iii) the Data User can establish sufficient grounds to retain the said personal data; or (iv) the Data User is required by its regulators (e.g. Bank Negara Malaysia, Securities Commission) to continue maintaining the said personal data.

Rationale: This section permits Data Users to retain personal data for more than seven years after non-approval/closure/termination. It satisfies P7-I3 because it specifies a minimum retention period threshold (>= 7 years) with conditional compliance paths.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 3.5

126. P7-I3 · s. 3.5.4

Finding key: e5d39e99b7856b222d4626261888e1818ff11c56827d251a09162a7bece0a7a7

Law: Personal Data Protection Code Of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

until a period of seven (7) years has elapsed from the date of non-approval, closure or termination of the said accounts/facilities.

Context: 3.5.4 As a general rule, Data Users are permitted to retain the personal data of Data Subjects from the date of application for the opening of accounts/facilities with Data Users, until a period of seven (7) years has elapsed from the date of non-approval, closure or termination of the said accounts/facilities.

Rationale: s. 3.5.4 permits Data Users to retain personal data for a defined minimum duration. Maps to P7-I3 because it specifies a retention period of seven (7) years after non-approval/closure/termination.

127. P7-I3 · s. 24(2)

Finding key: 6afdc24501dc4c1d346b5764f14420bbdb131179fc64ca0099130cde38ced760

Law: Services Tax Act (Act 807) 2018 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

preserved for a period of seven years from the latest date to which the record relates

Context: (2) Any record kept under this section shall be— 28 Laws of Malaysia Act 807 (a) preserved for a period of seven years from the latest date to which the record relates; (b) in the national language or English language; and (c) kept in Malaysia, except as otherwise approved by the Director General and subject to such conditions as he deems fit.

Rationale: This section requires records kept under it to be preserved for at least seven years. Maps to P7-I3 because it clearly specifies a minimum retention period (7 years). Legal function: minimum data retention requirement.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 24 › item (2)

128. P7-I3 · s. 32(2)

Finding key: 7bd6bcdc733d89c00c6c5e85355db32400527d6ded735a9c36abda639601e47f

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

retained for a period of not less than six years from the date the cyber security service was provided;

Context: (2) The information referred to in subsection (1) shall be— (a) kept and maintained in the manner as may be determined by the Chief Executive; (b) retained for a period of not less than six years from the date the cyber security service was provided; and (c) produced to the Chief Executive at any time as the Chief Executive may direct. Laws of Malaysia 32 Act 854

Rationale: This s.32(2) requires retention of specified information for at least six years. It satisfies P7-I3 because it clearly sets a minimum data retention period (>= 6 years). Legal function: minimum retention requirement.

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 32 › item (2)

129. P7-I4 · s. 12

Finding key: fe5f2bad2efeab6853a18e7b7a81836a764055173c2301c8793a24950bff3404

Law: Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1727) · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

“Seseorang pengawal data boleh memindahkan mana-mana data peribadi seorang subjek data ke”

Context: Seksyen 129 Akta ibu dipinda— (a) dengan memotong subseksyen (1); (b) dalam subseksyen (2)— (i) dengan menggantikan perkataan “Bagi maksud subseksyen (1), Menteri boleh menentukan” dengan perkataan “Seseorang pengawal data boleh memindahkan mana-mana data peribadi seorang subjek data ke”; dan (ii) dalam perenggan (a), dengan memotong perkataan “, atau yang dapat digunakan bagi maksud yang sama seperti Akta ini”; Undang-Undang Malaysia 10 Akta A1727 (c) dalam subseksyen (3)— (i) dengan menggantikan perkataan “subseksyen (1)” dengan perkataan “subseksyen (2)”; (ii) dalam perenggan (f), dengan memasukkan perkataan “atau” di hujung perenggan itu; (iii) dalam perenggan (g), dengan menggantikan perkataan “; atau” di hujung perenggan itu dengan noktah; dan (iv) dengan memotong perenggan (h); (d) dengan memotong subseksyen (4); dan (e) dalam subseksyen (5), dengan menggantikan perkataan “subseksyen (1)” dengan perkataan “seksyen ini”. Pindaan seksyen 136

Rationale: This section permits data controllers to transfer personal data. Maps to P7-I4 because it amends the transfer rule, not DPIA/DPO duties; the known-anchor text does not establish accountability mechanisms.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 12

Status evidence

  "status": "in_force",

130. P7-I5 · s. 46(1)

Finding key: d1f0a449009a136ae83cc528cbb148b967db49771226ff06aaa29e46b08b3f18

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Any authorized officer conducting a search under this Act shall be given access to computerized data whether stored in computer or otherwise.

Context: (1) Any authorized officer conducting a search under this Act shall be given access to computerized data whether stored in computer or otherwise.

Rationale: This section requires access be given to an authorized officer during a search. Maps to P7-I5 because it establishes government access to personal data without any stated independent judicial authorization requirement.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 46 › item (1)

131. P7-I5 · s. 114

Finding key: f51772cc8f12ca04bd6b10e8b938d4707fe0fad088b81fdf04a9fe60ae4e73ba

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

the authorized officer may enter the premises and exercise in, upon and in respect of the premises all the powers referred to in section 113 in as full and ample a manner as if he were authorized to do so by a warrant issued under that section.

Context: If an authorized officer is satisfied upon information received that he has reasonable cause to believe that by reason of delay in obtaining a search warrant under section 113 the investigation would be adversely affected or evidence of the commission of an offence is likely to be tampered with, removed, damaged or destroyed, the authorized officer may enter the premises and exercise in, upon and in respect of the premises all the powers referred to in section 113 in as full and ample a manner as if he were authorized to do so by a warrant issued under that section. Access to computerized data

Rationale: This section permits warrantless access. Maps to P7-I5 because it authorizes an officer to enter and access data without prior independent judicial authorization when delay would harm the investigation or evidence.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 114

132. P7-I5 · s. 113(3)

Finding key: 7fb4bd5e1c154ec1625ec5a33efec94255d4ac7869a7488599aeb1cd379b5916

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

An authorized officer conducting a search under subsection (1) may, for the purpose of investigating into the offence, search any person who is in or on the premises.

Context: (3) An authorized officer conducting a search under subsection (1) may, for the purpose of investigating into the offence, search any person who is in or on the premises.

Rationale: This provision permits an authorized officer to search a person. Maps to P7-I5 because it authorizes government access to personal data via search without any requirement for independent judicial authorization.

134. P7-I5 · s. 40

Finding key: 4e13ffc477e3fcadf903a370250f29f03f2fa322811305cc02c91df2f8d2453b

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

the authorized officer may enter the premises and exercise in, upon and in respect of the premises all the powers referred to in section 39 in as full and ample a manner as if he were authorized to do so by a warrant issued under that section.

Context: If an authorized officer is satisfied upon information received that he has reasonable cause to believe that by reason of delay in obtaining a search warrant under section 39 the investigation would be adversely affected or evidence of the commission of an offence is likely to be tampered with, removed, damaged or destroyed, the authorized officer may enter the premises and exercise in, upon and in respect of the premises all the powers referred to in section 39 in as full and ample a manner as if he were authorized to do so by a warrant issued under that section. List of seized objects, etc.

Rationale: This section permits warrantless investigative entry and search powers. Maps to P7-I5 because it authorizes government access to personal data without prior independent judicial authorization, subject only to exigency conditions. Function: conditional warrantless search/access.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 40

135. P7-I5 · s. 38(2)

Finding key: 5ab623e853846a0aba2c1f36b44106dfc7d466c5edc39f5aa9697fd0fd62ea41

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

Search and seizure with warrant

Context: (2) An authorized officer shall have the powers of a police officer of whatever rank as provided for under the Criminal Procedure Code [Act 593] in relation to investigation into any offence under this Act, and such powers shall be in addition to and not in derogation of, the powers provided for under this Act. Search and seizure with warrant

Rationale: This provision establishes search and seizure with warrant. Maps to P7-I5 because the access power is conditioned on judicial authorization, so it does not create warrantless government access.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 38 › item (2)

136. P7-I5 · s. 6(1)

Finding key: 0ba7232dc7eca50b78ff054631fb314bd0f7388f0d697932db5545cfd575dd36

Law: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

may authorize any police officer— (a) to intercept, detain and open any postal article in the course of transmission by post;

Context: (1) Notwithstanding any other written law, the Public Prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the commission of a security offence, may authorize any police officer— (a) to intercept, detain and open any postal article in the course of transmission by post; (b) to intercept any message transmitted or received by any communication; or (c) to intercept or listen to any conversation by any communication. 10 Laws of Malaysia Act 747

Rationale: This section permits the Public Prosecutor to authorize police to intercept, detain and open postal articles. Maps to P7-I5 because government access to personal data is allowed without independent judicial authorization; the trigger is prosecutorial discretion, not a court order.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 6 › item (1)

137. P7-I5 · s. 6(3)

Finding key: df98d6d42683d37227c14fa970bbf2c5bcbcbb3d92bb0a8481c530d6f0099262

Law: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

may— (a) intercept, detain and open any postal article in the course of transmission by post; (b) intercept any message transmitted or received by any communication; or (c) intercept or listen to any conversation by any communication, without authorization of the Public Prosecutor

Context: (3) Notwithstanding subsection (1), a police officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police may— (a) intercept, detain and open any postal article in the course of transmission by post; (b) intercept any message transmitted or received by any communication; or (c) intercept or listen to any conversation by any communication, without authorization of the Public Prosecutor in urgent and sudden cases where immediate action is required leaving no moment of deliberation.

Rationale: This s. 6(3) permits a police officer to intercept/open postal articles and intercept/listen to communications. Maps to P7-I5 because it establishes government access to personal data without independent judicial authorization; the safeguard is Public Prosecutor authorization, not a court order/warr

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 6 › item (3)

138. P7-I5 · s. 46(2)

Finding key: 546da6a76b8a4f47e98a5a1ac7cdd833696f5d78e5ff46e2d8728cbaf2eeb048

Law: Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

an authorized officer shall be provided with the necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software or hardware and any other means required to enable comprehension of the computerized data

Context: (2) For the purposes of this section, an authorized officer shall be provided with the necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software or hardware and any other means required to enable comprehension of the computerized data. No cost or damage arising from seizure to be recoverable

Rationale: This section requires provision of access-enabling means to an authorized officer. Maps to P7-I5 because it facilitates government access to personal data without any independent judicial authorization requirement in the text.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 46 › item (2)

139. P7-I5 · s. 6(2)

Finding key: c2354759d3d86290b5c13768c8ec8a8058a2f4d7c44099949b8079fa540a00e8

Law: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

may— (a) require a communications service provider to intercept and retain a specified communication or communications of a specified description received or transmitted, or about to be received or transmitted by that communications service provider;

Context: (2) The Public Prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the communication of a security offence, may— (a) require a communications service provider to intercept and retain a specified communication or communications of a specified description received or transmitted, or about to be received or transmitted by that communications service provider; or (b) authorize a police officer to enter any premises and to install on such premises, any device for the interception and retention of a specified communication or communications of a specified description and to remove and retain such evidence.

Rationale: This section permits the Public Prosecutor to require interception and retention of specified communications. It maps to P7-I5 because it authorizes government access to personal data without any independent judicial authorization in the text. Function: interception/retention order.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 6 › item (2)

140. P7-I5 · s. 45(2)

Finding key: 4584db1e9fc2cbd33278866dfab964b9718adb472e3793703eb96a7d2702f41b

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

personal data— (a) processed for— (i) the prevention or detection of crime or for the purpose of investigations; (ii) the apprehension or prosecution of offenders; or (iii) the assessment or collection of any tax or duty

Context: (2) Subject to section 46, personal data— (a) processed for— (i) the prevention or detection of crime or for the purpose of investigations; (ii) the apprehension or prosecution of offenders; or (iii) the assessment or collection of any tax or duty or any other imposition of a similar nature, shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act; (b) processed in relation to information of the physical or mental health of a data subject shall be exempted from the Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act of which the application of the provisions to the data subject would be likely to cause serious harm to the physical or mental health of the data subject or any other individual; (c) processed for preparing statistics or carrying out research shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act, provided that such personal data is not processed for any other purpose and that the resulting statistics or the results of the research are not made available in a form which identifies the data subject; (d) that is necessary for the purpose of or in connection with any order or judgement of a court shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act; 46 ACT 709 Laws of Malaysia (e) processed for the purpose of discharging regulatory functions shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act if the application of those provisions to the personal data would be likely to prejudice the proper discharge of those functions; or (f) processed only for journalistic, literary or artistic purposes shall be exempted from the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle, Retention Principle, Data Integrity Principle and Access Principle and other related provisions of this Act, provided that— (i) the processing is undertaken with a view to the publication by any person of the journalistic, literary or artistic material; (ii) the data user reasonably believes that, taking into account the special importance of public interest in freedom of expression, the publication would be in the public interest; and (iii) the data user reasonably believes that in all the circumstances, compliance with the provision in respect of which the exemption is claimed is incompatible with the journalistic, literary or artistic purposes. Power to make further exemptions

Rationale: This section exempts certain personal data from the Access Principle for law-enforcement and tax purposes. Maps to P7-I5 because it permits access-related processing without any requirement of independent judicial authorization.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 45 › item (2)

141. P7-I5 · s. 121

Finding key: 37447b2f45d5473c32a764dcaf9f118eee540757909802559bbdd6951ba73470

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

An authorized officer shall, for the purposes of the execution of this Act, have the power to do all or any of the following: (a) to require the production of any computer, book, account, computerized data or other document kept by the data user or any other person and to inspect, examine and to download from them, make copies of them or take extracts from them;

Context: An authorized officer shall, for the purposes of the execution of this Act, have the power to do all or any of the following: (a) to require the production of any computer, book, account, computerized data or other document kept by the data user or any other person and to inspect, examine and to download from them, make copies of them or take extracts from them; (b) to require the production of any identification document from any person in relation to any act or offence under this Act; 80 ACT 709 Laws of Malaysia (c) to make such enquiries as may be necessary to ascertain whether the provisions of this Act have been complied with. Power to require attendance of persons acquainted with case

Rationale: This section establishes compulsory inspection/production powers for an authorized officer. Maps to P7-I5 because it permits government access to computerized data/documents without any independent judicial authorization or warrant requirement.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 121

142. P7-I5 · s. 6(4)

Finding key: 2f530d0472d497d8379bc38cb2a2a5b3f896c020a548ca83c611c6f887d8f9fa

Law: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

he shall then be deemed to have acted under the authorization of the Public Prosecutor

Context: (4) If a police officer has acted under subsection (3), he shall immediately inform the Public Prosecutor of his action and he shall then be deemed to have acted under the authorization of the Public Prosecutor.

Rationale: This subsection establishes deemed authorization for police action. Maps to P7-I5 because it confirms access under Public Prosecutor authorization, not independent judicial authorization, so it is not a court-order-only safeguard.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 6 › item (4)

143. P7-I5 · s. 115(1)

Finding key: 1a09b3d6ff66bc9bbaf19cd0ecfacc4a11b92aba195d61585013e64dd2494d6a

Law: Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

An authorized officer conducting a search under sections 113 and 114 shall be given access to computerized data whether stored in a computer or otherwise.

Context: (1) An authorized officer conducting a search under sections 113 and 114 shall be given access to computerized data whether stored in a computer or otherwise.

Rationale: This section requires that an authorized officer be given access to computerized data during a search under ss. 113–114. It maps to P7-I5 because it establishes government access to personal data without stating any independent judicial authorization for that access.

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 115 › item (1)

144. P7-I5 · s. 29

Finding key: e0c000100afa07d8ddd578d2be36b4285634f4e91bd3f6f240bc90aab2f4ec70

Law: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

a police officer conducting an investigation under this Act shall be allowed to have access to any person whom he has reason to believe to be involved in a security offence who is— (a) being detained under any other written law; or (b) under confinement in prison, whether convicted or not.

Context: Notwithstanding any other written law, a police officer conducting an investigation under this Act shall be allowed to have access to any person whom he has reason to believe to be involved in a security offence who is— (a) being detained under any other written law; or (b) under confinement in prison, whether convicted or not. Detention pending exhaustion of legal process

Rationale: This section permits a police officer to access persons involved in a security offence when they are detained or confined. It maps to P7-I5 because it allows access without requiring independent judicial authorization (no warrant/court order condition).

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 29

145. P7-I5 · s. 60(2)

Finding key: 166f744b84aa31c7573369f502e8f4b7907ba9d20860ce32119e3face38dfc9c

Law: Services Tax Act (Act 807) 2018 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

may, if it is necessary so to do— (a) break open any outer or inner door of the place, premises or conveyance and enter every part thereof, if necessary forcibly;

Context: (2) The senior officer of service tax authorized under subsection (1) may, if it is necessary so to do— (a) break open any outer or inner door of the place, premises or conveyance and enter every part thereof, if necessary forcibly; (b) remove by force any obstruction to the entry to search or to seize as he is empowered to effect; and (c) detain every person found in the place, premises or conveyance until the search has been completed. 52 Laws of Malaysia Act 807 Search may be made without warrant

Rationale: This section permits a senior officer of service tax to forcibly enter and search premises/conveyances. It maps to P7-I5 because it establishes warrantless government access; no independent judicial authorization is required.

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 60 › item (2)

146. P7-I5 · s. 61

Finding key: b222eec5c8b95482dfea4123489c8ecb8e5b9e71b6434fc90005c21ed1c9dfb9

Law: Services Tax Act (Act 807) 2018 · Tag: NEW

Official source · Status: in_force

he may exercise in, upon and in respect of the place, premises or conveyance, all the powers under section 60 in the same manner as if he was empowered so to do by a warrant issued under that section.

Context: Whenever it appears to the senior officer of service tax that there is reasonable cause to believe that in any place, premises or conveyance there are concealed or deposited any goods, documents, articles or things which may afford evidence of the commission of any offence under this Act and if he has reasonable grounds for believing that by reason of the delay in obtaining a search warrant the goods, documents, articles or things are likely to be removed, he may exercise in, upon and in respect of the place, premises or conveyance, all the powers under section 60 in the same manner as if he was empowered so to do by a warrant issued under that section. Seizure of goods, etc.

Rationale: This section permits the senior officer of service tax to exercise search/seizure powers without first obtaining a warrant when delay risks removal. Maps to P7-I5 because it authorizes government access to personal data/evidence without independent judicial authorization. Function: warrantless searc

Discovery comparison: NEW · Citation path: section 61

148. P6-I2 · s. 77(2)

Finding key: 79f7e5acaca066adbbdd70a3e540c859024cc9e5f0df6604a4dc74d1312cd14c

Law: My Health Records Act 2012 · Tag: KNOWN

Official source · Status: in_force

to hold and take such records outside Australia, provided that the records do not include:

Context: (1) The System Operator, a registered repository operator, a registered portal operator or a registered contracted service provider that holds records for the purposes of the My Health Record system (whether or not the records are also held for other purposes) or has access to information relating to such records, must not: (a) hold the records, or take the records, outside Australia; or (b) process or handle the information relating to the records outside Australia; or (c) cause or permit another person: (i) to hold the records, or take the records, outside Australia; or (ii) to process or handle the information relating to the records outside Australia. (2) Despite subsection (1), the System Operator is authorised, for the purposes of the operation or administration of the My Health Record system: (a) to hold and take such records outside Australia, provided that the records do not include: (i) personal information in relation to a healthcare recipient or a participant in the My Health Record system; or (ii) identifying information of an individual or entity; and (b) to process and handle such information outside Australia, provided that the information is neither of the following: (i) personal information in relation to a healthcare recipient or a participant in the My Health Record system; (ii) identifying information of an individual or entity. Fault ‑ based offence (2A) A person commits an offence if the person contravenes subsection (1). Penalty: Imprisonment for 5 years or 300 penalty units, or both. Note: Where a fault element for a physical element of an offence is not stated, see section 5.6 of the Criminal Code for the appropriate fault element. Civil penalty (2B) A person is liable to a civil penalty if the person contravenes subsection (1). Civil penalty: 1,500 penalty units. (3) This section does not limit the operation of section 99.

Rationale: This section permits the System Operator to hold and take records outside Australia subject to content-based limits. Maps to P6-I2 because it authorises domestic storage of a copy/record while allowing transfer, i.e. a local-storage requirement with exceptions.

Discovery comparison: KNOWN · Citation path: section 77 › item (2)
